source.title: Separate visual pathways for perception and action

confidence_rationale: Goodale & Milner (1992) is the canonical paper establishing the dorsal/ventral visual stream distinction. The dorsal stream (V1â†’MTâ†’posterior parietal cortex) processes spatial location, egocentric coordinates, and action-relevant information at high temporal resolution. The ventral stream (V1â†’V4â†’inferotemporal cortex) processes object identity, allocentric relationships, and sustained perceptual representations. This anatomical distinction directly grounds MECH-096: the observation encoder requires two output heads corresponding to these streams, not a single encoder with a learned routing gate. A learned gate is insufficient because the two streams process structurally different information at different temporal resolutions â€” collapsing them degrades the z_self/z_world split back toward z_gamma conflation.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The primate visual system contains two anatomically distinct processing streams diverging from primary visual cortex. The dorsal stream (V1â†’MTâ†’posterior parietal cortex) is specialized for processing visuospatial information in egocentric coordinates for the purpose of action guidance â€” 'where/how'. The ventral stream (V1â†’V4â†’inferotemporal cortex) is specialized for processing object identity, shape, and colour for recognition and perceptual representation â€” 'what'. These streams process different aspects of the visual scene, operate at different temporal scales (dorsal: fast, transient; ventral: slower, sustained), and remain anatomically separate throughout the cortical hierarchy. Patient DF (visual form agnosia) can grasp objects she cannot recognize, demonstrating functional dissociation.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-096: the observation encoder must implement a dorsal-equivalent head (egocentric, action-relevant, high temporal resolution â†’ z_self) and a ventral-equivalent head (allocentric, object-identity, sustained â†’ z_world). These cannot be replaced by a single encoder with a learned routing gate because: (1) they process structurally different input features at different temporal scales; (2) end-to-end training with a single encoder will re-merge them toward z_gamma conflation under gradient pressure; (3) the biological evidence shows these streams remain anatomically distinct throughout the cortical hierarchy â€” their separation is structural, not learned. SD-005's design sketch ('encoder routes to appropriate stream') must be specified as two separate encoder heads to be anatomically faithful.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Goodale & Milner's distinction was developed for primate visual processing. REE operates on arbitrary observation spaces (not necessarily visual). The structural principle â€” that egocentric/action-relevant and allocentric/identity-relevant features require separate processing channels â€” generalises beyond vision, but the specific temporal resolution differences and anatomical separation may not directly transfer to all modalities. The 'two heads' requirement is a structural inference, not directly stated in the source.
mapping.source_context: Foundational review reinterpreting the Ungerleider & Mishkin (1982) 'what/where' distinction as 'what/how'; incorporates lesion evidence, single-unit recording, and the landmark patient DF case. Became the canonical framework for action-oriented vs perceptual visual processing.

confidence_components.notes: Extremely high source quality â€” canonical result with decades of replication. Mapping fidelity is good: the egocentric/allocentric split maps cleanly to z_self/z_world. Transfer risk is moderate because REE's observation space is not exclusively visual and the temporal resolution claim requires generalisation beyond the visual domain.
